claim: TDP-43 loss of function causes cryptic exon inclusion in UNC13A, reducing UNC13A protein and impairing synaptic vesicle release
notes: Brown et al 2022, Nature. TDP-43 depletion exposes a cryptic exon in UNC13A intron 20-21. UNC13A is essential for synaptic vesicle priming. Loss explains early synaptic dysfunction in ALS.

claim: UNC13A risk SNP rs12973192 creates a stronger TDP-43 binding site, making cryptic exon inclusion more severe and accelerating ALS progression
notes: Ma et al 2022, Nature. The common GWAS risk variant in UNC13A enhances cryptic exon inclusion specifically when TDP-43 is depleted. Explains how a common variant modifies ALS severity.

claim: TDP-43 depletion causes STMN2 cryptic exon inclusion and loss of stathmin-2 protein, impairing axonal regeneration
notes: Klim et al 2019, Melamed et al 2019, Nature Neuroscience. Concurrent discovery by two groups. STMN2 is essential for axonal maintenance and regeneration. TDP-43 loss → STMN2 loss → axonal degeneration.

claim: ASO targeting STMN2 cryptic exon restores stathmin-2 expression and axonal regeneration capacity in TDP-43 depleted neurons
notes: Baughn et al 2023 and related work. Steric-blocking ASO prevents cryptic exon inclusion, restoring full-length STMN2 mRNA and protein. Multiple groups developing toward clinical translation.

claim: ASO targeting UNC13A cryptic exon restores UNC13A expression in TDP-43 depleted neurons, rescuing synaptic function
notes: Demonstrated in Brown et al 2022 and subsequent work. Steric-blocking ASO approach mirrors STMN2 strategy. Potential synergy: dual STMN2 + UNC13A ASO could address both axonal and synaptic deficits.

claim: Pridopidine Phase 2 showed 32% slowing of ALSFRS-R decline, 62% respiratory benefit, and 57% survival improvement in ALS
notes: Prilenia Therapeutics Phase 2 trial results. Sigma-1R agonist. All-subtype ALS population. Led to PREVAiLS Phase 3 trial design. Multiple secondary endpoints positive.

claim: PREVAiLS Phase 3 trial of pridopidine enrolling 500 patients across 60 centers globally, starting January 2026
notes: Largest pridopidine trial. All-subtype ALS. Primary endpoint: combined functional + respiratory. HIGHLY RELEVANT for Erik — sporadic ALS, enrolling now.

claim: Sigma-1 receptor is an ER-mitochondria tethering protein that regulates calcium homeostasis, ER stress response, and proteostasis — all disrupted in ALS
notes: Hayashi & Su 2007, reviewed extensively. SIGMAR1 at MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) regulates IP3R calcium signaling, ERAD, and autophagy. Motor neurons particularly dependent on MAM function.

claim: SIGMAR1 mutations cause juvenile ALS (ALS16), confirming sigma-1 receptor as a genuine ALS disease gene
notes: Al-Saif et al 2011. Homozygous SIGMAR1 mutations in consanguineous family with juvenile ALS. Confirms that sigma-1R loss-of-function is sufficient for motor neuron disease.

claim: Rapamycin-induced autophagy clears TDP-43 aggregates and improves motor function in TDP-43 transgenic mouse models
notes: Wang et al 2012 and related work. mTOR inhibition activates autophagy, promoting clearance of TDP-43 aggregates. Dose-response relationship observed. Translational gap: optimal dosing for CNS autophagy without systemic immunosuppression.

claim: TFEB overexpression enhances autophagy-lysosomal pathway and reduces TDP-43 pathology in cellular and animal models
notes: TFEB is master transcription factor for lysosomal biogenesis. Activation promotes aggregate clearance. Multiple approaches to TFEB activation under investigation (mTOR-dependent and independent).

claim: Trehalose activates autophagy through mTOR-independent pathways and reduces protein aggregation in neurodegenerative disease models
notes: Sarkar et al 2007, J Biol Chem. Trehalose induces autophagy independently of mTOR, potentially complementing rapamycin. Oral bioavailability limited by intestinal trehalase. Favorable safety profile as natural disaccharide.

claim: XBP1 deficiency is paradoxically protective in ALS mice by enhancing autophagy, suggesting UPR modulation as therapeutic strategy
notes: Hetz et al 2009, Genes Dev. XBP1 conditional knockout in nervous system enhanced autophagy and reduced SOD1 aggregation. Reveals that some ER stress responses are maladaptive in ALS.

claim: UBQLN2 mutations cause X-linked ALS/FTD with ubiquitin-positive inclusions, implicating the ubiquitin-proteasome system in ALS
notes: Deng et al 2011, Nature. UBQLN2 delivers ubiquitinated substrates to proteasome. Mutations impair proteasomal degradation, causing accumulation of ubiquitinated aggregates. Links UPS dysfunction to ALS.

claim: FUS liquid droplets undergo time- and mutation-dependent hardening into solid fibrillar aggregates, establishing phase transition as disease mechanism
notes: Patel et al 2015, Cell. In vitro reconstitution showing FUS LLPS→solid transition. ALS mutations (R521C, R521H, P525L) dramatically accelerate hardening. Defines therapeutic window for phase transition intervention.

claim: Stress granule proteins with low-complexity domains undergo aberrant phase transitions from functional liquid condensates to pathological solid aggregates
notes: Molliex et al 2015, Mol Cell. hnRNPA1 and other SG proteins with LCD/prion-like domains undergo LLPS that can mature into amyloid-like fibrils. ALS mutations in multiple SG proteins accelerate this transition.

claim: C9orf72 dipeptide repeat proteins (poly-GR, poly-PR) disrupt membraneless organelles including stress granules and nucleoli
notes: Lee et al 2016, Cell Reports. Arginine-rich DPRs (poly-GR, poly-PR) promiscuously interact with LCD-containing proteins, disrupting phase-separated compartments. Explains widespread cellular dysfunction in C9orf72-ALS.

claim: C9orf72 repeat expansions disrupt nucleocytoplasmic transport (NCT), impairing nuclear import/export of proteins and RNA
notes: Zhang et al 2015, Freibaum et al 2015, Nature. Independent studies demonstrating NCT defects in C9orf72 models. DPRs and repeat RNA both contribute. RanGAP1 mislocalization observed.

claim: RanGAP1 nuclear transport protein is mislocalized in C9orf72 and sporadic ALS motor neurons, contributing to TDP-43 nuclear loss
notes: Zhang et al 2015. RanGAP1 mislocalization at nuclear pore impairs Ran-GTP gradient needed for nuclear import. May explain TDP-43 nuclear clearance even in sporadic ALS without C9orf72 expansion.

claim: OPTN mutations impair selective autophagy/mitophagy, accumulating damaged mitochondria and protein aggregates in ALS motor neurons
notes: Maruyama et al 2010, Nature. OPTN is a selective autophagy receptor. ALS mutations abolish its ability to recruit autophagic machinery to ubiquitinated substrates and damaged mitochondria.

claim: VCP/p97 mutations cause multisystem proteinopathy (ALS/FTD/myopathy/Paget's) by impairing ER-associated degradation and autophagy
notes: Johnson et al 2010. VCP/p97 AAA-ATPase is central to multiple proteostasis pathways. Mutations cause TDP-43 proteinopathy across multiple tissues. Links proteostasis failure to TDP-43 aggregation.

claim: TDP-43 accumulates in mitochondria, impairs complex I activity, and causes mitochondrial dysfunction in ALS motor neurons
notes: Wang et al 2016, Nature Medicine. Cytoplasmic TDP-43 localizes to mitochondria, binds mRNAs encoding respiratory complex subunits, and impairs oxidative phosphorylation. Links TDP-43 pathology to bioenergetic failure.

claim: TDP-43 depletion from the nucleus impairs DNA damage repair, causing genomic instability in ALS motor neurons
notes: Mitra et al 2019, Nature Commun. TDP-43 recruited to DNA double-strand breaks for NHEJ repair. Nuclear loss impairs DNA repair fidelity, accumulating genomic damage. Adds DNA damage to the cascade of TDP-43 loss consequences.
